hints.to lose: 'to lose' is 'amittere' — do not confuse with 'amare'

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'amo' — 'amavi' with -avi ending is perfect
hints.Imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'amabam' — 'amavi' is perfect indicative
hints.Pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'amaveram' — 'amavi' is perfect indicative

hints.amitto, amittere, amisi, amissum: These are the parts of 'amittere' (to lose) — not related to 'amare'
hints.audio, audire, audivi, auditum: These are the parts of 'audire' (to hear) — a 4th conjugation verb
hints.ambulo, ambulare, ambulavi, ambulatum: These are the parts of 'ambulare' (to walk) — a different 1st conjugation verb

hints.Nominative: While the form is identical, Augustine is addressing beauty directly — this is vocative of exclamation
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'pulchritudinem' — 'pulchritudo' is vocative here
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'pulchritudine' — Augustine addresses beauty directly in the vocative

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'pulchritudo, pulchritudinis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'pulchritudo' with -tudo suffix is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'pulchritudo' is 3rd declension

steps.prompt: What does 'sero' mean in this context?

hints.Seriously: 'Seriously' would be 'serio' — 'sero' here is an adverb meaning 'late'
hints.I sow: 'sero' can mean 'I sow' as a verb, but here it is the adverb meaning 'late'

hints.A comparison: 'more ... than ...': Comparisons use 'magis ... quam ...' — 'tam ... et tam ...' is correlative emphasis
hints.A concession: 'although ... yet ...': Concessions use 'quamvis ... tamen ...' — 'tam' means 'so / so much'
hints.A condition: 'if ... then ...': Conditions use 'si ... tum ...' — 'tam' here intensifies both adjectives equally

steps.prompt: What rhetorical effect does Augustine achieve by juxtaposing 'antiqua' and 'nova'?
steps.target: Paradox — God's beauty is both ancient and ever-new
choices: Paradox — God's beauty is both ancient and ever-new

hints.Contradiction showing confusion: Augustine is not confused — the juxtaposition is a deliberate theological paradox about God's nature
hints.A chronological narrative: This is not a narrative sequence — it is a simultaneous paradoxical description of divine beauty
hints.Hyperbole for dramatic effect: This is not exaggeration — Augustine means both literally: God's beauty is truly ancient and truly new

translation: Late have I loved you, beauty so ancient and so new.
